topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. A profile picture loaded: a face I hadn't seen in fifteen years, a smirk that used to feel like a physical punch. It was Mark. My childhood tormentor. My stomach tightened instantly, a familiar, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I stared at the request, my thumb hovering over the 'Accept' button, the silence of my apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Why now? After all this time, why reach out? I deleted the message, the impulse to delete warring with a strange, unwelcome curiosity. The empty space on the screen felt suddenly vast, echoing the hollow feeling that had settled in my chest all day.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at my phone, half-expecting a notification that wasn't there. The apartment was too big; the shadows stretched long across the floorboards, making the familiar furniture seem alien and distant. When the ping finally appeared, a jolt of unwelcome anxiety shot through me. It was him. The sheer audacity of it made my breath hitch. I traced the name on the screen with a shaky finger, feeling a profound sense of isolation settle over me, heavy and suffocating. I didn't move. I just sat there, the silence of the room pressing in, waiting for a reason that probably wouldn't come. My shoulders slumped, a physical manifestation of the weight I was carrying.

[story 3]

The sound of the notification was sharp in the otherwise muted atmosphere of my room. I picked up the device slowly, my hand trembling slightly. Seeing his name pop up brought a wave of unwelcome recollection—the stinging words, the enforced exclusion. A strange mixture of apprehension and a dull ache washed over me. I scrolled through his profile, noting the updates, the curated facade. I felt a deep, pervasive emptiness inside, the kind that makes even simple tasks feel monumental. I finally closed the app, the screen going dark. I leaned my head against the cool glass of the window, watching the streetlights blur, wishing for any kind of disturbance, any sign that something real was happening outside this sealed-off space.
